elements.sent-2411-001: High-quality material synthesis is essential for replicating complex surface properties to create realistic digital scenes.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, existing methods often suffer from inefficiencies in time and memory, require domain expertise, or demand extensive training data, with high-dimensional material data further constraining performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: Additionally, most approaches lack multi-modal guidance capabilities and standardized evaluation metrics, limiting control and comparability in synthesis tasks.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these limitations, we propose \ours, a novel neural material synthesis framework utilizing hyperdiffusion.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method employs neural fields as a low-dimensional representation and incorporates a multi-modal conditional hyperdiffusion model to learn the distribution over material weights.
elements.sent-2411-006: This enables flexible guidance through inputs such as material type, text descriptions, or reference images, providing greater control over synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-007: To support future research, we contribute two new material datasets and introduce two BRDF distributional metrics for more rigorous evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-008: We demonstrate the effectiveness of \ours through extensive experiments, including a novel statistics-based constrained synthesis approach, which enables the generation of materials of desired categories.
elements.sent-2411-009: Material synthesis plays a crucial role in visual computing, enabling the creation of realistic material appearances for applications in rendering, augmented reality, and scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-010: Analytic models~\cite{phong, cook-torrance, ggx are widely used to encode material appearance, due to their speed, memory efficiency, and ease of editing, which makes them suitable for real-time applications.}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, their simplified representations often fail to capture the complexity of real-world materials, limiting their reconstruction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-012: In contrast, measured data, while achieving unparalleled realism, is memory-intensive and challenging to manipulate, which restricts its flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-013: Implicit neural representations (INRs) offer a promising alternative, combining high-quality reconstructions with memory efficiency and a speed comparable with analytic models~\cite{sztrajman2021nbrdf .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, the lack of intuitive editing in INRs continues to hinder their usability and general adoption.
elements.sent-2411-015: Deep-learning-based methods have also been explored and proven effective in generating highly realistic materials~\cite{Chen2023Text2TexTT, gatys2015texture, zhou2018non .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Nevertheless, they typically require extensive amounts of synthetic material data for training.
elements.sent-2411-017: Furthermore, their performance and efficiency are constrained by the high dimensionality of material data, which complicates the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-018: To overcome these challenges, our proposed pipeline, \ours, introduces two strategies for effective data augmentation and employs implicit neural representations (INRs) as a low-dimensional, continuous representation of materials.
elements.sent-2411-019: This approach simplifies the learning process, enabling the model to capture the underlying material distribution more efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-020: In addition, current material synthesis methods lack robust quantitative metrics for evaluating synthesis quality, making it difficult to assess and compare different approaches -- unlike generative models in other fields~\cite{theis2016evalgen, betzalel2022evalgen .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Another limitation is the absence of multi-modal conditioning, which would enable users to guide the synthesis process using diverse inputs, such as material type, text descriptions, or reference images.
elements.sent-2411-022: This limitation reduces the flexibility and control available to artists and designers, hindering the alignment of synthesized output with user intent.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address these limitations, we propose a set of novel BRDF distributional metrics and leverage a multi-modal conditional hyperdiffusion model to support flexible user input.
elements.sent-2411-024: The main contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-025: A novel material synthesis pipeline using a multi-modal conditional hyperdiffusion model that supports user-specified material generation via material type, natural language descriptions, or image references;.
elements.sent-2411-026: Two new material datasets: AugMERL, an enhanced collection of tabulated BRDF values, and NeuMERL, a dataset of materials represented through implicit neural fields; and.
elements.sent-2411-027: A thorough evaluation of \ours{ ’s effectiveness, demonstrated through an extensive analysis that includes a set of novel BRDF distributional metrics and a novel constrained generation experiment to synthesize materials of desired categories.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Capturing real-world material appearance requires lengthy acquisition times and substantial storage.
elements.sent-2411-029: Traditional capture uses four-axis gonioreflectometers~\cite{gonio, White98 and other system setups~\cite{weinmann2015advances, haindl2013visual, danabtf, weinmann-2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Matusik \etal developed a high-resolution BRDF database with 100 isotropic materials~\cite{Matusik2003datadriven .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Filip and Vávra contributed a database of 150 materials, many exhibiting anisotropic properties~\cite{filip2014template .}
elements.sent-2411-032: These material databases are continuously being augmented by later methods~\cite{ngan2005experimental, dupuy2018adaptive.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Material appearance has been widely represented by the bidirectional reflectance distribution function~(BRDF)~\cite{Nicodemus1977GeometricalCA, Guarnera2016BRDFsurvey, montes2012overview, ngan2005experimental, westin2004comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-034: While analytical BRDF models~\cite{phong, cook-torrance, ggx, disney offer efficient reconstruction and editing, their simplified assumptions limit the representation of complex real-world materials~\cite{ngan2005experimental, guarnera2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Data-driven approaches offer higher realism~\cite{Matusik2003datadriven , although they are often hard to manipulate and require large storage.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Dimensionality reduction techniques can alleviate this issue, but at the expense of compromising material quality~\cite{lawrence2004, nielsen2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Recently, deep learning methods provide efficient, low-dimensional BRDF representations with high-quality reconstruction~\cite{deepbrdf, zheng, sztrajman2021nbrdf, gokbudak2023hypernetworks, Fanlayered, metalayered .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Our work leverages a neural field architecture~\cite{sztrajman2021nbrdf for efficient, realistic material modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Material synthesis based on analytic models has been widely explored~\cite{zhang2024textmat, memery2023materialNLP, Chen_2023_ICCV, Chen2023Text2TexTT, hu2023generating, tchapmi2022generating .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For data-driven representations, previous works have resourced to various strategies, including dimensionality reduction~\cite{Matusik2003datadriven, abdi2010principal , perceptual mappings~\cite{nielsen2015, serrano2016, sun2018connecting and deep learning~\cite{deepbrdf, gokbudak2023hypernetworks : Hu~\etal~\cite{deepbrdf used a convolutional autoencoder to learn a low-dimensional manifold from a measured BRDF database, enabling material synthesis and editing.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, their BRDF representation is constrained to a fixed resolution with high storage requirements.
elements.sent-2411-042: Similar to us, Gokbudak~\etal~\cite{gokbudak2023hypernetworks leveraged a hypernetwork architecture to predict the weights of a neural fields representation of material appearance.}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, their approach requires sample measurements of BRDF data as input and is thus limited to sparse reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this work, we introduce an approach for data-driven material generation based on a multi-modal hyperdiffusion architecture.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our method generates continuous and low-dimensional representations of material appearance, and can be conditioned on images, material categories and natural language descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-046: An overview of our novel material synthesis pipeline, \ours, is shown in \cref{fig:pipeline_overview , consisting of three main stages.}
elements.sent-2411-047: First, we augment the MERL dataset through RGB permutation and PCA interpolation, generating the Augmented MERL (AugMERL) dataset (\cref{sec:Data Augmentation ).}
elements.sent-2411-048: Next, we adopt neural fields as a low-dimensional, continuous representation for materials, fitting them to individual materials in AugMERL to create a new dataset of neural material representations, Neural MERL (NeuMERL).
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, we train a transformer-based, multi-modal hyperdiffusion model on NeuMERL to capture the complex distribution of neural materials, enabling high-fidelity and diverse synthesis through unconditional, multi-modal conditional, and constrained generation (\cref{sec:Experiments ).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/architecture.pdf} \caption{An overview of \ours, our novel neural material synthesis framework, consisting of three main stages. 1 (top left): Data augmentation using RGB permutation and PCA interpolation to create an expanded dataset, AugMERL; 2 (middle): Neural field fitted to individual materials, resulting in NeuMERL, a dataset of neural material representations; and 3 (bottom): Training a multi-modal conditional hyperdiffusion model on NeuMERL to enable conditional synthesis of high-quality, diverse materials guided by inputs such as material type, text descriptions, or reference images. We further propose a novel statistics-based constrained synthesis method to generate materials of a specified type (top right).} \label{fig:pipeline_overview}
elements.sent-2411-050: We utilize the MERL BRDF dataset~\cite{Matusik2003datadriven , which includes 100 materials, each represented by densely sampled BRDF values in a $D_\text{MERL =90 \times 90 \times 180$-dimensional space.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Through experimentation, we determined that 100 samples are insufficient for effective hyperdiffusion training.
elements.sent-2411-052: To address this, we augment the MERL dataset using RGB permutation and PCA interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-053: First, we permute the three color channels (RGB) of each MERL sample, yielding an expanded dataset of $100 \times 3! = 600$ samples.

elements.sent-2411-061: Neural fields provide a low-dimensional, continuous representation for material data.
elements.sent-2411-062: Following prior work~\cite{sztrajman2021nbrdf , we overfit a compact neural field $f_r^{\rv{\xi }$ parameterized by $\rv{\xi}$, to each material in AugMERL.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Once fitted, we treat the flattened weights of each neural field as the material's neural representation.
elements.sent-2411-064: With 2400 materials in AugMERL, this process yields a dataset of 2400 neural material representations, which we refer to as Neural MERL (NeuMERL).
elements.sent-2411-065: Representing materials as flattened 1D vectors enables a flexible framework for modeling complex distributions, abstracting away the underlying data’s dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-066: This approach makes our pipeline adaptable to diverse data formats.
elements.sent-2411-067: In the following section, we detail three key techniques employed in fitting the neural fields.
elements.sent-2411-068: In our preliminary experiments and other studies~\cite[\eg,][]{zhou2024physically , it is observed that directly using the conventional BRDF input format -- namely the incident and outgoing directions $\rv{\omega _i, \rv{\omega _o \in \mathbb{R ^3$ -- can complicate the fitting process for certain materials and occasionally introduce undesirable artifacts possibly due to the high dimensionality of the input.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: To address this, we employ the Rusinkiewicz reparametrization~\cite{rusinkiewicz1998 , which defines the half and difference vectors $\rv{h}$ and $\rv{d}$ as follows: \begin{equation}\rv{h} := \frac{\rv{\omega}_i + \rv{\omega}_o}{\lVert \rv{\omega}_i + \rv{\omega}_o \rVert}; \rv{d} := R_{\hat{\rv{b}},-\theta_\rv{h}} R_{\hat{\rv{n}},-\varphi_\rv{h}} \rv{\omega}_i,\end{equation} where $R_{\rv{v , \alpha}$ denotes a rotation around the vector $\rv{v}$ by the angle $\alpha$, $\hat{\rv{n }$ is the surface normal, and $\hat{\rv{b }$ is the surface binormal.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: This reparametrization helps improve the robustness of the neural field fitting process by addressing reciprocity constraints more directly~\cite{zhou2024physically .}
elements.sent-2411-071: We then proceed by adopting the spherical coordinates of the half and difference vectors $\rv{h}$ and $\rv{d}$, specifically $\theta_{\rv{h , \varphi_{\rv{h , \theta_{\rv{d , \varphi_{\rv{d }$, as inputs to our neural fields.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: A further advantage of using the Rusinkiewicz reparametrization is that, since our materials are isotropic, the BRDF remains invariant with respect to $\varphi_{\rv{h }$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Consequently, we can omit this parameter, reducing the input complexity from $(\rv{\omega _i, \rv{\omega _o) \in \mathbb{R ^6$ to $(\theta_{\rv{h , \theta_{\rv{d , \varphi_{\rv{d ) \in [0, \frac{\pi {2 ]^2 \times [0, \pi)$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: This reparametrization enhances the efficiency of our neural field representation without sacrificing accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-075: The high dynamic range of BRDF values makes fitting reflectance data particularly sensitive to error distribution.
elements.sent-2411-076: For low reflectance values, even minor fitting errors can have a large impact on the loss, causing shifts in perceived ``hue'' in rendered images, which leads to unrealistic colors and reduced visual fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-077: To address these issues, we employ a mean absolute logarithmic loss for BRDF values: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\text{NF}(\rv{\xi}) := \mathbb{E}_{ \raisebox{-4mm}{ $\mathclap{ \substack{ \theta_{\rv{h}}, \theta_{\rv{d}}, \varphi_{\rv{d}} } }$ } }\mkern-5mu \Big[ \left| \log \left(1 + f_r \cos\theta_i \right) - \log \left(1 + f_r^{\rv{\xi}} \cos\theta_i \right) \right| \Big]\end{equation} where \( f_r \) denotes the ground-truth BRDF, which \( f_r^{\rv{\xi \) aims to approximate, and \( \theta_i \) is the polar angle of the incident direction.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: This loss is computed per color channel, offering a balanced approach that stabilizes training across samples with both low and high values.
elements.sent-2411-079: Consequently, it enhances the model’s capability to manage dynamic reflectance variations, leading to more realistic color reproduction and improved visual fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-080: Ideally, the neural materials in NeuMERL should originate from a consistent distribution.
elements.sent-2411-081: However, due to a phenomenon known as weight symmetry~\cite{bengio2011expressive , we observe that different weight configurations can yield the same neural field.}
elements.sent-2411-082: For instance, swapping weights between two neurons in a hidden layer, or flipping the signs of both input and output weights for a neuron before an odd, linear, or piecewise-linear activation function like ReLU~\cite{nair2010rectified , results in an identical neural field.}
elements.sent-2411-083: To address this, we propose using the optimized weights from the first fitted neural field as the initialization for subsequent neural field fittings.
elements.sent-2411-084: This approach helps align the weights across all fitted fields, promoting consistency within the NeuMERL dataset and facilitating smoother training of the hyperdiffusion model in the next stage.

elements.sent-2411-085: To model the complex distribution within the NeuMERL dataset, we utilize a diffusion process~\cite{ho2020denoisingdiffusion, peebles2022learning, erkoc2023hyperdiffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Specifically, we employ a transformer-based denoising network~\cite{vaswani2017attention , leveraging its demonstrated efficacy~\cite{peebles2022learning and its attention mechanisms allowing for an effective focus on relevant information, enhancing the network’s ability to capture intricate dependencies in the data.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Our hyperdiffusion supports conditioning across three modalities: material type (represented as integers), text description, and reference images.
elements.sent-2411-088: For each modality, we utilize a categorical encoding for material type, an augmented CLIP text embedding~\cite{radford2021learning, zhou2023clip for text, and a ResNet~\cite{he2016deep for images.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: This multi-modal conditioning approach enables material synthesis to be guided by different user inputs, enhancing workflow intuitiveness and accessibility and allowing for a more smooth and accurate translation of creative vision into generated materials.
elements.sent-2411-090: For conditional sampling, we employ classifier-free guidance (CFG)~\cite{ho2022cfg .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Please refer to \cref{sec:more-diffusion-model,sec:Transformer Backbone in Hyperdiffusion,sec:attention-module,sec:Conditional Sampling with Classifier-Free Guidance in the supplementary for further details on the diffusion model, transformer, attention mechanism, and CFG, respectively.}

elements.sent-2411-095: We fit neural fields to individual materials in the AugMERL dataset (\cref{sec:Data Augmentation ), which is derived from the MERL BRDF dataset~\cite{Matusik2003datadriven .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Our hyperdiffusion model is trained on the NeuMERL dataset (\cref{sec:NBRDF Fitting ), which consists of neural material representations.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The training-validation split is 80\%-20\% for AugMERL and 95\%-5\% for NeuMERL.
elements.sent-2411-098: In the constrained synthesis experiments in \cref{sec:Constrained Synthesis , statistical information is gathered from the MERL dataset~\cite{Matusik2003datadriven .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: We use Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)~\citep{heusel2017gans as an image-based metric to assess the quality of rendered single-view images by comparing them to a reference set.}
elements.sent-2411-100: To the best of our knowledge, effective metrics for directly comparing material distributions are still lacking.
elements.sent-2411-101: Drawing inspiration from distributional metrics for point clouds~\cite{achlioptas2018learning, lopez2016revisiting, yang2019pointflow , we introduce three novel material distributional metrics -- minimum matching distance (MMD), coverage (COV), and 1-nearest neighbor (1-NNA) -- to evaluate the fidelity and diversity of synthesized BRDF sets $\mathcal{S}$ relative to a reference set $\mathcal{R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Each metric is based on an underlying distance measure $d(f_r, f_r')$ between two BRDFs.
elements.sent-2411-103: MMD measures the average distance from each reference BRDF to its nearest synthesized counterpart: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\text{MMD}^d(\mathcal{R}, \mathcal{S}) := \frac{1}{\left|\mathcal{R} \right|} \sum_{f_r \in \mathcal{R}} \underset{f_r' \in \mathcal{S}}{\min} d(f_r, f_r')\end{equation} MMD evaluates the fidelity of the synthesized set relative to the reference, with a lower score indicating higher fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-104: COV calculates the proportion of reference BRDFs that are ``covered'' by the synthesized set.
elements.sent-2411-105: A reference BRDF is considered covered if it is the closest neighbor to at least one synthesized BRDF: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\text{COV}^d(\mathcal{R}, \mathcal{S}) = \frac{\left| \left\{ \underset{f_r \in \mathcal{R}}{\text{argmin }} d(f_r, f_r') \mid f_r' \in \mathcal{S} \right\} \right|}{\left|\mathcal{R} \right|}\end{equation} COV assesses the diversity of the synthesized set, with a higher score reflecting better coverage.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}L_COV^d(R, S) = \frac{\left| \left\{ \underset{f_r \in R}{argmin } d(f_r, f_r') \mid f_r' \in S \right\} \right|}{\left|R \right|}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: In this metric, each sample is classified as belonging to either the reference set \(\mathcal{R}\) or the synthesized set \(\mathcal{S}\) based on the membership of its nearest neighbor.
elements.sent-2411-107: If \(\mathcal{R}\) and \(\mathcal{S}\) are drawn from the same underlying distribution, the classifier's accuracy will approach 50\% with a large sample size.
elements.sent-2411-108: An accuracy closer to 50\% indicates greater similarity between \(\mathcal{R}\) and \(\mathcal{S}\), suggesting that the model has effectively learned the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-109: Each distributional metric can be computed using an underlying distance \(d\).
elements.sent-2411-110: Potential options include rendering-based metrics such as root mean squared error (RMSE), peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), and structural similarity index measure (SSIM)~\cite{SSIM04 .}
elements.sent-2411-111: PSNR measures the reconstruction quality of images, while SSIM predicts perceived similarity.
elements.sent-2411-112: Since higher values in PSNR and SSIM correspond to greater similarity, we negate these values -- resulting in NegPSNR and NegSSIM, respectively -- to make them plausible distance functions\footnote{Although NegPSNR and NegSSIM may not strictly meet the criteria for distance metrics (\eg, violating positivity), they suffice to provide useful relative distance measures for material distributional metrics.. .}
elements.sent-2411-113: To directly assess the distance between two BRDFs without relying on renderings, we also introduce the following BRDF-L1 distance: \begin{equation}d_BRDF-L1 := \underset{\theta_{\rv{h}}, \theta_{\rv{d}}, \varphi_{\rv{d}}}{E} \Big[ \left| f_r - f_r' \right| \Big];\end{equation} For further background on the image-based metrics and validation of the proposed material distributional metrics, please refer to \cref{sec:Metric Details in the supplementary.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We begin by presenting the results of unconditional synthesis of neural materials using \ours.
elements.sent-2411-115: Most existing approaches utilize principal component analysis (PCA)~\cite{abdi2010principal, sun2018connecting and variational autoencoders (VAEs)~\cite{kingma2013auto, gokbudak2023hypernetworks .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Accordingly, we developed four generative baselines based on the two methods PCA and VAE.
elements.sent-2411-117: Each method was applied to either the AugMERL or NeuMERL datasets, resulting in the following baselines: PCA-A, PCA-N, VAE-A, and VAE-N.
elements.sent-2411-118: For synthesis, PCA-based baselines perform random linear interpolation in the principal space, while VAE-based baselines sample from a standard Gaussian prior.
elements.sent-2411-119: Additional background details on these methods can be found in \cref{sec:variational-autoencoder,sec:PCA-Based Baselines in the supplementary material.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The quantitative and qualitative results indicate that \ours consistently outperforms all baselines across metrics, producing diverse, high-quality, visually appealing, and perceptually realistic renderings.
elements.sent-2411-121: This demonstrates the effectiveness of \ours for neural material synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-122: Notably, materials synthesized by the PCA- and VAE-based baselines exhibit significant artifacts, likely due to the limitations of these simpler models in capturing the complex distribution of neural materials.

elements.sent-2411-123: To further evaluate the effectiveness of our pipeline, we perform multi-modal conditional synthesis by conditioning our model on various modalities of input: material type, text description, or material images.

elements.sent-2411-164: The attention mechanism~\cite{vaswani2017attention allows models to focus on specific parts of input data, dynamically assigning different levels of ``attention'' or importance to different elements.}
elements.sent-2411-165: Attention mechanisms enable models to learn which parts of the input sequence are most relevant to predicting each output token.
elements.sent-2411-166: This process improves performance by allowing models to prioritize relevant information and ignore irrelevant details, especially in long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-167: In modern applications, attention modules are widely used in natural language processing (NLP), computer vision, and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-168: The transformer architecture~\cite{vaswani2017attention , which relies heavily on the self-attention mechanism, has become foundational in NLP models, including BERT~\cite{devlin2018bert and GPT~\cite{mann2020language .}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Attention allows these models to capture complex dependencies between words in a sentence, regardless of their distance from each other, leading to significant advancements in tasks like language translation, sentiment analysis, and image processing.
elements.sent-2411-170: The attention mechanism computes a weighted combination of values based on the relevance of each value to a given query.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the context of self-attention (or scaled dot-product attention) in transformer models~\cite{vaswani2017attention , this process involves three main components: queries ($\rv{q}$), keys ($\rv{k}$), and values ($\rv{v}$).}
elements.sent-2411-172: In summary, the attention mechanism dynamically focuses on relevant parts of the input by computing similarity scores between queries and keys, normalizing these scores, and using them to weight the values.
elements.sent-2411-173: This allows models to capture dependencies across elements in a sequence, making attention a powerful tool for handling long-range dependencies in data.
elements.sent-2411-174: Given an input sequence of embeddings $\rv{X}$ (\eg, word embeddings in NLP or patch embeddings in vision), we first transform it into three different linear projections: \begin{equation}\rv{q} = \rv{X}W_\rv{q}; \rv{k} = \rv{X}W_\rv{k}; \rv{v} = \rv{X}W_\rv{v},\end{equation} where \( W_\rv{q \), \( W_\rv{k \), and \( W_\rv{v \) are learnable weight matrices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: These projections represent the queries, keys, and values, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-176: The attention score between each query and key is then computed as the dot product \( \rv{q \cdot \rv{t \).}}
elements.sent-2411-177: This results in a matrix of scores that represents the similarity between each element in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-178: To stabilize gradients and prevent large values in the dot-product computation, the scores are scaled by the square root of the dimensionality of the queries/keys \( \sqrt{d_\rv{k \), where \( d_\rv{k \) is the dimension of the key vectors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: The scaled scores are passed through a softmax function, producing attention weights that sum to 1.
elements.sent-2411-181: This step converts the scores into probabilities, indicating the relevance of each value with respect to each query.
elements.sent-2411-182: These attention weights are used to compute a weighted sum of the values.
elements.sent-2411-183: Specifically, the output of the attention mechanism is \begin{equation}f_\text{attention}(\rv{q}, \rv{k}, \rv{v}) = f_\text{softmax}\left(\frac{\rv{q} \cdot \rv{k}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \rv{v}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-184: This produces a context vector for each query that incorporates information from all other elements in the sequence, weighted by their relevance.

elements.sent-2411-188: We adopt variational autoencoders (VAEs)~\cite{kingma2013auto as one of the baseline models in \cref{sec:unconditional-synthesis .}}
elements.sent-2411-189: VAEs are probabilistic generative models designed to capture the underlying probability distribution of a given dataset $\mathcal{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-190: A VAE consists of a parameterized encoder $\vaeenc$ and decoder $\vaedec$, defined by parameters $\rv{\psi}$ and $\rv{\zeta}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-191: Assuming that the latent variables $\rv{z \in \mathbb{R ^{D_\rv{z }$ follow a prior distribution $\vaeprior$, both the encoder and decoder are jointly optimized to maximize the evidence lower bound (ELBO) on the likelihood of the data: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_\text{ELBO} (\rv{\psi}, \rv{\zeta}; \rv{x}) := \, \mathbb{E}{\vaeenc} \left[ \log \vaedec \right] - \mathcal{D}_\text{KL} \left( \vaeenc, \vaeprior \right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D _\text{KL}$ represents the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the approximate posterior and the prior distribution~\cite{csiszar1975divergence .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: This probabilistic framework enables VAEs to effectively approximate the true data distribution, facilitating robust generative modeling of complex data.
elements.sent-2411-193: We adopt K-means clustering in constrained synthesis, where unconditional materials are filtered out by statistical information rather than a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-194: K-means clustering is a commonly used unsupervised method to partition \(n\) data samples \(\rv{X \in \mathbb{R ^{n\times p}\) into \(k\) clusters \(\mathcal{S =\{S_1, ..., S_{k}\}\), minimizing the distance between each sample and its center \begin{equation}\mu_i = \frac{1}{|S_i|} \sum_{\mathbf{x_i}\in S_i}\mathbf{x_i}.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Conditional sampling with classifier-free guidance (CFG)}\label{alg:ddim} \begin{algorithmic} \Require total timestep $T$ \Require guidance scale $\omega \geq -1$ \Require conditional context $\rv{y}$ \Require variance schedule $\{\beta_t\}_{t=1}^T$ \State $\rv{x}_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_{D_\text{NF}})$ \Comment{sample $\rv{x}_T$ from prior} \For{$t=T$ to $1$} \State $\rv{\epsilon}_\rv{\eta}^\text{CFG}(\rv{x}_t, \rv{y}, t) = (1+\omega)\rv{\epsilon_\eta}(\rv{x}_t, \rv{y}, t) - \omega \rv{\epsilon_\eta}(\rv{x}_t, \emptyset, t)$ \State $\alpha_t = \prod_{i=1}^t \sqrt{1-\beta_i}$ \State $\gamma_t = \prod_{t=1}^{t} \alpha_t$ \State $\rv{x}_t^\text{CFG} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\gamma_{t}}}(\rv{x}_t - \sqrt{1- \gamma_{t}} \rv{\epsilon_\eta^\text{CFG}}(\rv{x}_t, \rv{y}, t))$ \State $\rv{x}_{t-1}= \sqrt{\gamma_{t-1}} \rv{x}_t^\text{CFG} + \sqrt{1-\gamma_{t-1}} { \rv{\epsilon_\eta^\text{CFG}}(\rv{x}_t, \rv{y}, t)}$ \EndFor \State \Return $\rv{x}_0$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-237: SSIM~\cite{SSIM04 is a perception-based metric that measures the perceptual similarity of the two images.}
elements.sent-2411-238: The computation of SSIM is based on three comparison measurements between the two images: luminance ($l$), contrast ($c$), and structure ($s$) defined as \begin{equation}l(I_1, I_2) := \frac{2 \mu_x \mu_y + c_1}{\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + c_1}; c(I_1, I_2) := \frac{2 \sigma_{I_1} \sigma_{I_2} + c_2}{\sigma_{I_1}^2 + \sigma_{I_2}^2 +c_2 }; s(I_1, I_2) := \frac{\sigma_{I_1 I_2} + c_3}{\sigma_{I_1} \sigma_{I_2} + c_3},\end{equation} respectively, where $\mu_I, \sigma_I$, and $\sigma_{I_1I_2}$ are the mean, standard deviation, and variance of the images: \begin{equation}\mu_I := \frac{1}{w \cdot h} {\sum_{i=1}^{w} \sum_{j=1}^{h} I(i,j) }; \sigma_I := \sqrt{ \frac{1}{w \cdot h- 1} {\sum_{i=1}^{w} \sum_{j=1}^{h} \left( I(i,j)-\mu_I \right)^2 }}; \sigma_{I_1I_2} := \frac{1}{w \cdot h - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{w} \sum_{j=1}^h \left(I_1(i,j)-\mu_{I_1})(I_2(i,j)-\mu_{I_2} \right)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}c_1 := (k_1 L)^2; c_2 := (k_2 L)^2; c_3 := \frac{c_2}{2}\end{equation} are the variables to stabilize the division with weak denominator where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are coefficients defaulted to 0.01 and 0.03, respectively, and $L$ is the dynamic range of the pixel-values typically chosen to be $2^l-1$ where $l$ is the number of bits per pixel.

elements.sent-2411-306: Drawing inspiration from the metrics for point clouds~\cite{yang2019pointflow , we introduce three novel material distributional metrics -- minimum matching distance (MMD), coverage (COV), and 1-nearest neighbor (1-NNA) -- to evaluate the fidelity and diversity of synthesized BRDF sets $\mathcal{S}$ relative to a reference set $\mathcal{R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-307: MMD measures the average distance from each reference BRDF to its nearest synthesized counterpart: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\text{MMD}^d(\mathcal{R}, \mathcal{S}) \coloneqq \frac{1}{\left|\mathcal{R} \right|} \sum_{f_r \in \mathcal{R}} \underset{f_r' \in \mathcal{S}}{\min} d(f_r, f_r')\end{equation} MMD evaluates the fidelity of the synthesized set relative to the reference, with a lower score indicating higher fidelity.

elements.sent-2411-351: The computation of SSIM is based on three comparison measurements between the two images: luminance ($l$), contrast ($c$), and structure ($s$) defined as \begin{equation}l(I_1, I_2) := \frac{2 \mu_1 \mu_2 + c_1}{\mu_1^2 + \mu_2^2 + c_1}; c(I_1, I_2) := \frac{2 \sigma_{I_1} \sigma_{I_2} + c_2}{\sigma_{I_1}^2 + \sigma_{I_2}^2 +c_2 }; s(I_1, I_2) := \frac{\sigma_{I_1 I_2} + c_3}{\sigma_{I_1} \sigma_{I_2} + c_3},\end{equation} respectively, where $\mu_I, \sigma_I$, and $\sigma_{I_1I_2}$ are the mean, standard deviation, and variance of the images: \begin{equation}\mu_I := \frac{1}{w \cdot h} {\sum_{i=1}^{w} \sum_{j=1}^{h} I(i,j) }; \sigma_I := \sqrt{ \frac{1}{w \cdot h- 1} {\sum_{i=1}^{w} \sum_{j=1}^{h} \left( I(i,j)-\mu_I \right)^2 }}; \sigma_{I_1I_2} := \frac{1}{w \cdot h - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{w} \sum_{j=1}^h \left(I_1(i,j)-\mu_{I_1})(I_2(i,j)-\mu_{I_2} \right)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}c_1 := (k_1 L)^2; c_2 := (k_2 L)^2; c_3 := \frac{c_2}{2}\end{equation} are the variables to stabilize the division with weak denominator where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are coefficients defaulted to 0.01 and 0.03, respectively, and $L$ is the dynamic range of the pixel-values typically chosen to be $2^l-1$ where $l$ is the number of bits per pixel.
